sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, LEFT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 59, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 58 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 55 actions returned the board to a state already seen 55 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 55 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 4 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over! The level completed after pressing UP (the 3rd action in my batch). Let me check the result.
Looking at the output, after UP the bar blocks changed position: b stayed at (19,37) and S moved to (20,37). And then the game ended.
So the sequence that completed the level was RIGHT, LEFT, UP. But wait - the game says "The game is over" which could mean level completed OR game over (failed). Let me check the last_action_result.
Actually, looking at the message "The game is over" - this typically means the level/level is done. Let me check if it's level_completed or game_over.
The key finding is that arrow keys control the bar blocks, and pressing UP completed the level. The grid being all white (reset) is just the result of the level completion.
Now I need to move to the next level. Let me check the current state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check if level was completed
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}")
print(f"Current level: {current_frame.level}")
print(f"Current step: {current_frame.step}")
print(f"Valid actions: {valid_actions}")

# Check grid state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nGrid cells:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 9 and n['id'] > 5:
        bnd = n['boundary']
        r = sum(p[0] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        c = sum(p[1] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        print(f"  ({r},{c}): color={n['color']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Level completed: False
Game over: True
Done: False
Current level: 1
Current step: 58
Valid actions: ['UP', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT', 'MOUSE']

Grid cells:
  (50,25): color=g
  (50,30): color=W
  (50,35): color=g
  (55,25): color=W
  (55,30): color=g
  (55,35): color=W
  (60,25): color=g
  (60,30): color=W
  (60,35): color=g

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over (game_over=True) but level_completed=False. This means I failed the level! The grid is back to the initial state with cross cells white and corners gray.
So the arrow keys don't solve the puzzle - they just move the bar blocks around. And pressing UP triggered a game over condition.
Let me reconsider. The game ended when I pressed UP. What was the bar state before UP? After LEFT, the bar blocks were at:
- b: (19,37) with 2 pixels
- S: (19,38) with 2 pixels
After UP:
- b: (19,37) with 2 pixels  
- S: (20,37) with 2 pixels
The S block moved down. And this triggered game over.
So the bar blocks are being controlled by arrow keys, and moving them in certain ways causes game over. The goal might be to move the bar blocks to a specific position.
But wait - I've been on level 1 for 58 actions. The efficient solution should be much shorter. Let me think about what I know:
1. The left panel has 4 purple dots in a cross pattern
2. The right panel is a 3x3 grid
3. Clicking purple dots resets cross cells to white
4. Clicking grid cells cycles their color
5. Arrow keys move the bar blocks
6. The bar has blue blocks on the left and sky blue/blue blocks on the right
Maybe the goal is to move the bar blocks to match some target position? Or maybe the grid pattern needs to match the left panel?
Let me try a completely fresh approach. The grid is now back to initial state. Let me try clicking the 4 cross cells to make them green (matching the 4 purple dots in the left panel), without clicking the center.
From my earlier experiments, I know that clicking 3 cross cells resets them all. But if I click the center cell first (to make it green), then I can click all 4 cross cells without resetting.
Wait, but the center is currently gray. Let me try:
1. Click center → green
2. Click top cross → green
3. Click left cross → green
4. Click right cross → green
5. Click bottom cross → green
This should give me all 5 cross cells green, matching the pattern if the left panel had 5 dots. But it only has 4.
Actually, let me reconsider. The left panel has 4 dots in a cross pattern. The grid has 4 cross cells. Maybe I need to click the 4 cross cells to make them green, matching the 4 purple dots. The trick is avoiding the reset.
From my experiments:
- Clicking 3 cross cells (when center is gray) causes reset
- Clicking 4 cross cells (when center is green) doesn't cause reset until all 5 are green
So the sequence is: click center first, then click 4 cross cells. This gives 5 green cells. Then I need to click the center again to turn it gray, leaving 4 green cross cells.
But earlier when I tried this (all 5 green, then click center), everything turned gray.
Let me try a different order: click 2 cross cells, click center, click remaining 2 cross cells. Then the center is green with 4 cross cells green. Then click center to turn it gray.
Actually, I already tried this and it caused a full reset.
Hmm, let me try yet another approach. What if I need to click the cross cells in a specific order? Like top, right, bottom, left (clockwise)?
Let me just try the simplest thing: click all 4 cross cells one by one and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game over state. Grid is back to initial: cross=WHITE, center=GRAY, corners=GRAY
# Let me try a new approach: click the 4 cross cells one by one to see the reset behavior
# First, click top cross
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 50, 'col': 30}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After click 1 (top):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 9 and n['id'] > 7:
        bnd = n['boundary']
        r = sum(p[0] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        c = sum(p[1] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        print(f"  ({r},{c}): color={n['color']}")

# Click right cross
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 35}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After click 2 (right):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 9 and n['id'] > 7:
        bnd = n['boundary']
        r = sum(p[0] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        c = sum(p[1] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        print(f"  ({r},{c}): color={n['color']}")

# Click bottom cross
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 60, 'col': 30}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After click 3 (bottom):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 9 and n['id'] > 7:
        bnd = n['boundary']
        r = sum(p[0] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        c = sum(p[1] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        print(f"  ({r},{c}): color={n['color']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
